source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.4.1-8.4.2 fstab and mounting (pp. 143-145)

questions.q: When is /etc/fstab read, and what does it contain?
options: At system startup; it lists the file systems to mount, one per line
Only when you run fdisk; it lists partitions
Every second by a daemon; it logs disk usage
At shutdown; it lists files to delete
questions.why: /etc/fstab is read at startup and describes each file system to mount on its own line.

questions.q: What are the six columns of an /etc/fstab line, in order?
options: device : mount point : FS type : options : dump(backup) : fsck order
mount point : device : options : FS type : fsck order : dump
device : FS type : mount point : dump : options : fsck order
UUID : label : size : options : dump : fsck order
questions.why: The fields are: filesystem device, mount point, filesystem type, mount options, dump/backup flag, and fsck check order.

questions.q: What does the sixth column of /etc/fstab control?
options: The order fsck checks the file system (0 = no check, 1 = priority, 2 = not priority)
Whether the filesystem is backed up
questions.why: Column 6 sets the fsck pass order; column 5 is the dump/backup flag.
mount <device> <directory> attaches the file system into the tree at that point.

questions.q: Mount ALL the file systems listed in /etc/fstab at once.
questions.why: mount -a mounts everything in /etc/fstab; the results are recorded in /etc/mtab.

questions.q: Unmount the file system mounted at /home.
questions.why: umount detaches a mounted file system, given either its mount point or its device.

questions.q: Why might umount fail with 'device is busy'?
options: Because your shell (or a process) is still under the mount point
Because the device does not exist
questions.why: You must not be positioned below the mount point (nor have processes using it) when unmounting, or you get 'device is busy'.
